narrative: We need to scale distributed manufacturing to support innovation and development in the US — ground systems infrastructure for aerospace, basic energy infrastructure, and cryogenic infrastructure for fusion and advanced energy.

items.tweet_text: @micsolana The NYC Department of Education spends more on education than Japan's Ministry of Education spends on the entire country of Japan.
items.context: Palmer Luckey highlights that New York City's Department of Education budget exceeds Japan's entire national education budget, illustrating massive US public spending inefficiency.
items.reception: Going viral at 11K likes+RT — massive resonance across tech, policy, and fiscal accountability circles.
items.caption: We spend more than anyone and build less than we should. Imagine if we redirected even a fraction of this bloat into workforce training for advanced manufacturing and skilled trades. That's how you rebuild industrial capacity. #ManufacturingUSA #DefenseIndustrial #Reshoring

items.tweet_text:
FFS.🤦‍♂️ What's next? Putting lead back in gasoline?

US EPA plans to loosen mercury rules for coal plants this week https://t.co/ohk66uNu4I
items.context: Jesse Jenkins criticizes the US EPA's planned loosening of mercury emissions rules for coal plants, framing it as a rollback of environmental protections.
items.reception: Strong traction at 3K likes+RT — high engagement from energy policy and environmental audiences.
items.caption: We don't have to choose between energy infrastructure and environmental standards. The future is building cleaner, harder-to-build systems — fusion, advanced nuclear, cryo infrastructure. That requires more manufacturing capacity, not less regulation. #EnergyInfrastructure #AdvancedEnergy #BuildBetter

items.tweet_text: @Aviation_Intel It has gotten so bad.  No accountability even at a personal level - it isn't just their editors that don't care, their fellow reporters don't care either.
items.context: Palmer Luckey laments the lack of accountability in journalism, noting that neither editors nor fellow reporters hold each other to standards on accuracy.
items.reception: Strong engagement at 1.8K likes+RT — resonates broadly with tech and defense audiences frustrated with media coverage.
items.caption: Same dynamic in defense reporting. Programs get mischaracterized, manufacturing realities get ignored, and nobody corrects the record. Hard to build public support for industrial capacity when the narrative is always wrong. #DefenseTech #Accountability

items.tweet_text: Today in @Nature, we shared new results from Project Silica—our work to encode data in glass—pushing toward durable, immutable archival storage designed to last for millennia. https://t.co/4PM6Jh1wXU
items.context: Satya Nadella announces new results from Microsoft's Project Silica, which encodes data in glass for ultra-durable archival storage lasting millennia, published in Nature.
items.reception: Solid traction at 1.2K likes+RT — strong signal from enterprise tech and research communities.
items.caption: Glass-based archival storage is a materials science and precision manufacturing challenge as much as a data problem. This is exactly the kind of advanced manufacturing the US needs to own end-to-end — from substrate fabrication to deployment. #AdvancedManufacturing #DeepTech #Innovation

items.tweet_text: This is really interesting, what happens when the math no longer works due to capital and the models have to differentiate.  How will they do it? https://t.co/X7LhiBHHfD
items.context: Eric Schmidt discusses what happens when the economics of training massive AI models stop working due to capital constraints and models must differentiate on something other than scale.
items.reception: Good engagement at 864 likes+RT — resonates with AI/VC/enterprise strategy audiences.
items.caption: When the models commoditize, the differentiation moves to the physical layer — custom silicon, cooling infrastructure, energy supply, and the manufacturing to deliver it all. Software eats the world until it hits a capacity constraint. #AIInfrastructure #Manufacturing #Cryogenics

items.tweet_text: Watching the PR comms from Rocket Lab and Blue Origin on Mars Telecommunications Orbiter has been fascinating. https://t.co/GwoseAbeJE
items.context: Eric Berger comments on the competing PR communications strategies from Rocket Lab and Blue Origin regarding NASA's Mars Telecommunications Orbiter contract.
items.reception: Solid niche engagement at 706 likes+RT — core space industry audience tracking procurement dynamics closely.
items.caption: Love the competition but the real question is who has the manufacturing pipeline to deliver on schedule. Comms orbiters need ground systems, cryo propulsion infrastructure, and tested supply chains. The PR is fun — the production readiness is what matters. #SpaceManufacturing #Aerospace #DefenseTech

items.tweet_text: It’s time to build the Space Age Renaissance. https://t.co/m7qaGOqZGM
items.context: Marc Andreessen calls for building a 'Space Age Renaissance,' linking to content advocating for ambitious space development.
items.reception: Strong engagement at 615 likes+RT — broad resonance across tech optimist and space industry circles.
items.caption: You don't get a Space Age Renaissance without an industrial base to support it. Launch vehicles, ground systems, cryogenic infrastructure, propulsion test facilities — all of it needs to be manufactured at scale, in the US. Time to build the factories, not just the vision. #SpaceAge #DistributedManufacturing #BuildInAmerica

items.tweet_text: Production runs on ECS. My lab runs Kubernetes because I wanted to see what the fuss was about. It’s like I built myself an infrastructure fidget spinner. https://t.co/pPxM6cUuTC
items.context: Corey Quinn jokes about running Kubernetes in his home lab despite using simpler ECS in production, calling Kubernetes an 'infrastructure fidget spinner.'
items.reception: Moderate engagement at 214 likes+RT — solid traction in DevOps/cloud infrastructure niche.
items.caption: Manufacturing has its own version of this — overengineered digital twins sitting next to whiteboards that actually run the shop floor. Complexity for its own sake doesn't scale production. Keep it simple, keep it building. #ManufacturingTech #Infrastructure

items.tweet_text:
For decades, so-called “free” trade hasn’t been free or fair to American workers.

It’s time to fight for Wisconsin by bringing back jobs, reshoring manufacturing, and producing more goods Made in America. https://t.co/wAcJ6HGeYA
items.context: Rep. Tom Tiffany advocates for reshoring manufacturing to Wisconsin, arguing decades of free trade policies have hurt American workers.
items.reception: Moderate engagement at 204 likes+RT — politically aligned audience with reshoring/industrial policy interest.
items.caption: This is exactly right. Reshoring isn't a slogan — it's a national security imperative. Aerospace ground systems, energy infrastructure, cryogenic supply chains — none of it should depend on foreign production. Wisconsin and states like it should be building this future. #Reshoring #MadeInAmerica #DefenseManufacturing

items.tweet_text: "How are we going to get to the Moon if we can't talk between two or three buildings?" https://t.co/ptRlKxUT98
items.context: Eric Berger highlights internal communication dysfunction at NASA, quoting frustration about the inability to coordinate across buildings while planning Moon missions.
items.reception: Niche but engaged at 168 likes+RT — space industry insiders nodding in recognition.
items.caption: This is what happens when you let institutional infrastructure decay for decades. It's not just IT — it's test facilities, manufacturing floors, and integration bays. You can't run a lunar program on 1990s industrial capacity. Rebuild the base first. #NASA #Aerospace #Infrastructure

items.tweet_text:
Need to reboot a company server but don’t want to wait for a maintenance window? Wait 20 minutes for the next GitHub outage, slap the reboot button, and send anyone who complains to the GitHub status page. 

#modernsysadminwisdom
items.context: Corey Quinn jokes about using GitHub outages as cover for rebooting company servers without waiting for a maintenance window.
items.reception: Moderate niche engagement at 163 likes+RT — DevOps humor audience.
items.caption: In manufacturing, the maintenance window IS production. You don't get to hide behind someone else's outage when a cryogenic system goes down. Uptime reliability in physical infrastructure is a different discipline entirely. #IndustrialOps #Manufacturing

items.tweet_text: Amish lifestyle of tradition on the farm, but for developers working like it’s 2020.
items.context: Joe Lonsdale quips that some developers are living like it's permanently 2020, stuck in remote/legacy work patterns, comparing it to Amish traditionalism.
items.reception: Moderate engagement at 155 likes+RT — tech culture commentary audience.
items.caption: Meanwhile in manufacturing, you can't build a pressure vessel from your couch. The future of distributed manufacturing needs people on floors, in facilities, running real hardware. The 2020 remote era doesn't apply when you're bending metal. #Manufacturing #SkillledTrades #BuildThings

items.tweet_text:
Vendors dislike the DownDetector approach, but I do prefer it as a quick “is something globally broken right now” spot check. 

I don’t care if it’s CloudFlare or AWS or a routing convergence issue; I just need to know if it’s localized to me or not. https://t.co/veNat2jB76
items.context: Corey Quinn defends DownDetector as a useful quick-check tool for determining whether an outage is local or global, despite vendor objections.
items.reception: Modest niche engagement at 134 likes+RT — infrastructure/SRE community.
items.caption: Supply chain needs its own DownDetector. When a critical cryogenic component supplier goes dark, you need real-time visibility — is it my PO or is the whole sector constrained? Distributed manufacturing demands distributed monitoring. #SupplyChain #Manufacturing

items.tweet_text: @nypost Tastes like they did to me.
items.context: Joe Lonsdale casually responds to a New York Post story (likely about food quality or a product), saying it tastes fine to him.
items.reception: Low engagement at 129 likes+RT — casual/off-topic interaction with minimal industry signal.

items.tweet_text:
Another week, another Software Factory release:

• Agent Framework UX Overhaul: Scroll control during streaming, real-time visible thinking tokens, and one-click navigation to retrieved sources

• Structured Question & Answer Tool: Agents pose questions via a Q&A form
items.context: Chamath Palihapitiya shares a weekly product update from his Software Factory, detailing UX improvements to an AI agent framework and a new structured Q&A tool.
items.reception: Modest engagement at 127 likes+RT — AI/SaaS product audience.
items.caption: Software factories ship weekly. Hardware factories should too. The cadence of iteration in software needs to translate into physical manufacturing — rapid tooling, modular production lines, continuous improvement cycles. That's how you scale defense and energy infrastructure. #SoftwareFactory #AdvancedManufacturing #DefenseTech

items.tweet_text: @xdNiBoR No one ever went poor betting on spaceflight delays.
items.context: Eric Berger jokes that betting on spaceflight delays is always a safe bet, commenting on the industry's chronic schedule slippage.
items.reception: Niche engagement at 108 likes+RT — core space industry humor audience.
items.caption: Delays aren't destiny — they're a symptom of underinvested manufacturing infrastructure. When you have one facility, one supplier, one test stand, every slip cascades. Distributed manufacturing and redundant capacity is how you compress timelines. #Aerospace #SpaceManufacturing #SupplyChain

items.tweet_text:
This year for Lent I’m giving up desserts and… I have to admit it… I have a sweet tooth. No Tillamook Vanilla Bean Ice Cream till Easter. 

But more importantly, the idea of Lent is to remember the ultimate sacrifice of Christ for each one of us.
items.context: Pat Gelsinger shares his Lenten commitment to give up desserts and reflects on the spiritual significance of Christ's sacrifice.
items.reception: Modest engagement at 80 likes+RT — personal/faith-based audience, limited industry relevance.

items.tweet_text: @GingerRoyalty @Devon_Eriksen_ @kiwitalkz Citing games from a decade ago makes his point, not yours.  GTAV in particular came out 13 years ago.
items.context: Palmer Luckey argues in a gaming discussion that citing decade-old games like GTA V actually supports the point that the gaming industry has stagnated in innovation.
items.reception: Low engagement at 60 likes+RT — niche gaming culture debate, limited industry crossover.
items.caption: The gaming industry's stagnation mirrors what happened to defense manufacturing. When your best examples are a decade old, you have an innovation problem. Sustained investment in new capacity and new technology is the only way to stay ahead. #Innovation #DefenseTech

items.tweet_text: @skenzyme You might want to read this official report from the Texas grid operator on what failed during Uri. Wind turbines had very very little to do with it. Over 30 gigawatts of forced outages at gas (25 GW) and coal (5 GW) units during Uri. That's >40% of the peak ERCOT demand during https://t.co/ZhDIlMzVa1
items.context: Jesse Jenkins corrects a claim blaming wind turbines for the 2021 Texas Winter Storm Uri grid failures, citing ERCOT data showing 30+ GW of forced outages were from gas and coal plants.
items.reception: Modest engagement at 58 likes+RT — energy policy niche audience.
items.caption: Uri wasn't a generation-type problem — it was an infrastructure hardening problem. Gas plants need winterized equipment. The grid needs resilient physical infrastructure. This is a manufacturing and construction challenge, not just a policy debate. #EnergyInfrastructure #GridResilience #Manufacturing
